critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with genuine attention to temporal leakage, calibration, and confound control. I'll organize my critique by root cause rather than severity.

---

### Root Cause 1: Preprocessing fitted on the full cohort before the temporal split

Section 4 states: 'Scaling and encoding transformers are parameterized once on the complete extracted cohort and then applied identically to the train, validation, and test partitions.'

This is a direct form of temporal leakage. The imputation medians, feature scales, and category vocabularies are computed using information from year 3 — the held-out period — and then back-applied to the training data. The stated justification ('so that differences between temporal cohorts are not conflated with differences in preprocessing') has the causality exactly backwards: if the test period has different distributions (which is the whole point of a temporal split), then applying a pooled scaler suppresses that drift. More concretely:

- Median imputation computed on the full cohort incorporates future lab reference ranges, medication patterns, and diagnosis coding practices. A model trained on this will be calibrated to the full-cohort median, not the training-period median it would see at deployment.
- Category vocabularies from year 3 ICD codes will include procedure/diagnosis codes introduced or changed in that period, meaning the training data has encoded categories that weren't defined when those admissions occurred.

This is not a minor calibration issue. For a 3-year span, EHR coding practices drift (ICD-10 updates, formulary changes, order set revisions). The preprocessing contamination means the baseline features — comorbidity indices, diagnosis presence/absence — are constructed using future coding conventions, potentially inflating the apparent stability of the feature space.

Evidence against: If the design were changed to fit preprocessing on training data only and apply forward, and performance were essentially unchanged, the leakage is immaterial. Evidence for: If test-period ICD or lab vocabularies differ meaningfully from training-period vocabularies (easily checkable), the leaked scaler/imputer is doing real work.

---

### Root Cause 2: Test set contamination via the 'confirmatory pass' selection step

Section 7 explicitly describes using the test set for model selection: 'If the top validation configuration and a nearby alternative are effectively tied on validation performance but diverge on this out-of-period check, the configuration with the more stable behavior on the test period will be retained. Once the specification is locked, the same test set is used to report primary and secondary metrics.'

This is a textbook violation of held-out test set integrity. The test set is used to break ties between model configurations and is then re-used as the primary evaluation surface. Even when framed as 'stability' selection rather than performance maximization, the procedure optimizes over the test set — it just optimizes a different objective (variance stability vs. point AUROC). The reported AUROC on the test set is no longer an unbiased estimate of generalization performance; it is an estimate conditional on having selected the model that performed better on that specific test period.

The underlying assumption — that 'stable behavior on the test period' is a property of the model rather than a property of the test period's case mix — is unverifiable from a single test set. A model configuration that happened to align well with H2-year-3 idiosyncrasies would be preferentially retained.

The design could salvage this by either: (a) using the test set strictly once after full specification lock with no selection contingent on test behavior, or (b) adding a second temporal holdout for final evaluation after selection. As written, the confidence intervals bootstrapped on the test set are anti-conservative because selection bias is not accounted for.

Evidence: The proportion of cases where the tie-breaking procedure would actually activate is unknown — if it almost never fires, the contamination is small. But the design does not commit to reporting when it activates, so the reader cannot assess the magnitude. This is not a hypothetical risk; the procedure as described guarantees at least some test-set-conditional selection.

---

### Root Cause 3: Confounding between model complexity and feature richness is not fully isolated

The baseline is logistic regression on baseline features; the hypothesis model is gradient boosted trees on all features. The design acknowledges this ('ensures any improvement is attributable to the richer feature set, not model complexity') but does not include the necessary control: a gradient boosted tree trained on baseline features only.

Without this cell, it is impossible to decompose the AUROC improvement into (a) feature gain and (b) model complexity gain. XGBoost can capture nonlinear interactions in age × comorbidity × prior utilization that logistic regression cannot, and these baseline-only interactions may be responsible for a meaningful fraction of any observed improvement. The ablation described (baseline + embeddings only, no sequences) tests feature subsets but always uses the same model family for hypothesis configurations, while keeping logistic regression as the sole baseline-architecture comparator.

The stated justification that logistic regression 'represents current clinical practice' is a deployment argument, not a methodological control. For the purpose of attributing improvement to feature content, the comparison requires matching the model family.

Evidence: If GBT trained on baseline features alone matches or closely approaches the hypothesis model's AUROC, the sequential/note features are adding little. If LR on baseline features and GBT on baseline features are close, the complexity confound is small. Neither comparison is currently planned.

---

### Root Cause 4: Clinical BERT embedding assumptions for 7-day readmission

The methodology uses a pretrained clinical BERT model on discharge summaries and final 24-hour notes, extracting mean/max/percentile statistics of the embedding vectors. Several implicit assumptions here deserve scrutiny:

1. **The pretrained BERT model's training period and the EHR extraction period may overlap.** If the clinical BERT was trained on notes from the same health system (a common scenario with models like ClinicalBERT trained on MIMIC or proprietary corpora), and if the test-period notes resemble that training corpus more than the training-split notes do, the embeddings carry forward-looking distributional information. The design does not document which clinical BERT variant is used or whether its training data temporally overlaps the study cohort.

2. **Mean/max/percentile pooling of BERT embeddings discards sequential structure.** For a task where the semantic trajectory of a note matters (e.g., 'initially stable, now deteriorating'), aggregation statistics are a lossy summary. The methodology uses this aggregation then credits the embedding features with signal — but the embedding features are dominated by topic distribution (what is mentioned) rather than clinical trajectory (how things changed). This is a limitation worth naming, not necessarily a flaw, but the interpretation of SHAP values on pooled embeddings will not distinguish these.

3. **Note length is included as a summary feature and acknowledged as a potential confounder.** The stratified analysis by note length is appropriate, but note length is correlated with admission complexity and documentation culture in ways that change over the 3-year study window. A model that learns 'longer notes → higher readmission risk' may be learning a coding/documentation practice pattern that was introduced mid-cohort, which would produce inflated AUROC that degrades rapidly post-deployment.

Evidence: Checking whether AUROC for the embedding features degrades when note length is residualized out (not just stratified) would test the third point. For the first point, documentation of the BERT model's provenance and training data period is required.

---

### Summary

Two of the four issues are genuine methodological flaws that affect the validity of the reported AUROC: the preprocessing leakage (Root Cause 1) and the test-set-conditional model selection (Root Cause 2). These are not minor — together they mean the reported test AUROC is biased upward by an unknown amount and does not represent the generalization performance the design claims to measure. Root Cause 3 is a missing baseline that prevents attribution of improvement to features vs. model class. Root Cause 4 is a set of implicit assumptions about the embedding features that affect interpretability and deployment stability.

The temporal split design, confound controls, and calibration targets are genuinely well-conceived. The flaws are concentrated in the preprocessing and final evaluation protocol.
defender_raw:
## Pass 1 — Analysis of Each Critique Point

### Critique 1: Preprocessing fitted on full cohort before temporal split (temporal leakage)

**Critic's claim:** Fitting scalers, imputers, and vocabulary encoders on the complete extracted cohort — including year 3 (validation + test) — constitutes temporal leakage. Future lab reference ranges, medication patterns, ICD coding conventions, and category vocabularies from the held-out period contaminate the training-period feature representations. This inflates apparent stability of the feature space and potentially biases the baseline features (comorbidity indices, diagnosis presence/absence) by using future coding conventions.

**Assessment:** This critique is substantively correct and I cannot adequately rebut it.

The stated rationale in Section 4 — 'so that differences between temporal cohorts are not conflated with differences in preprocessing' — does have a legitimate kernel of intent: we wanted consistent encoding across splits to avoid spurious preprocessing artifacts. However, the critic is right that the implementation has the causality backwards. The correct approach is to fit all preprocessing transformers exclusively on training data and apply them forward to validation and test. If year 3 introduces new ICD-10 codes or medication names not present in years 1–2, those categories should appear as 'unknown' or be mapped to the 'missing' category using training-fitted vocabularies — that is the realistic deployment scenario. Using a pooled vocabulary suppresses that drift and is a form of look-ahead. For continuous features, the pooled median incorporates future distributional shifts (e.g., a lab assay recalibration mid-cohort, a formulary change that alters which medications are common), meaning the training-fitted model is calibrated to a scaler it would never see in deployment.

The magnitude of this leakage is genuinely uncertain. If the 3-year EHR period is relatively stable in coding practice, the leaked information in the scaler may be minimal. But over a 3-year span spanning ICD-10 updates and formulary cycles, this is not a safe assumption — it is an empirical question. I concede this is a real methodological flaw that should be corrected: fit all preprocessing on training data only, apply forward.

**What remains uncertain:** How much this actually biases the reported AUROC. It is possible that for the continuous features (labs, LOS, age) the pooled vs. training-only median differs little; for categorical vocabularies the impact depends on how many new codes appear in year 3. The fix is straightforward and should be implemented, but the degree of upward bias from this specific flaw is unknown without running the corrected pipeline.

---

### Critique 2: Test set used for model selection before being re-used as primary evaluation surface

**Critic's claim:** Section 7 describes a 'confirmatory pass' where, if two configurations are tied on validation, the one with more stable behavior on the test period is retained — and then the same test set reports primary metrics. This is a textbook violation of held-out test set integrity. Even optimizing for 'stability' rather than point AUROC constitutes selection conditional on the test set, making the reported AUROC anti-conservative. The proportion of cases where this tie-breaking fires is undisclosed.

**Assessment:** This critique is correct. I cannot defend the procedure as written.

The intent was to guard against deploying a configuration that happened to overfit the validation half-year but would degrade on the subsequent half-year — a real concern in temporal settings. The underlying motivation is sound: we do not want to select a configuration that is brittle to the time gap between validation and test. However, the implementation corrupts the evaluation surface. Once the test set has been consulted — for any purpose, including stability assessment — the reported metrics on that same test set are no longer an unbiased estimate of generalization. The bootstrapped confidence intervals computed afterward do not account for the selection step and are therefore anti-conservative, as the critic correctly identifies.

The design should have either: (a) committed to a strict policy where the test set is never consulted until after full specification lock, accepting that the validation period alone governs model selection; or (b) reserved a third temporal holdout (e.g., H1 year 4 if data were available) for final evaluation after the test-set-informed selection. As written, neither option was taken.

I also concede the transparency problem: the design does not commit to reporting when the tie-breaking procedure activates, so an external reader cannot assess whether the primary reported AUROC was selected conditional on test-set performance or not. This is a material disclosure gap.

**What remains uncertain:** How often the tie-breaking procedure actually fires in practice. If configurations are rarely tied on validation, the contamination may be small. But 'rarely' is not 'never,' and the design provides no mechanism to verify or report this. The flaw is structural, not just probabilistic.

---

### Critique 3: Baseline model and hypothesis model differ in both feature set and model family

**Critic's claim:** Logistic regression (baseline features) vs. GBT (all features) confounds feature richness with model complexity. A missing cell — GBT trained on baseline features only — is needed to decompose AUROC improvement into feature contribution vs. complexity contribution. The stated justification that LR 'represents current clinical practice' is a deployment argument, not a methodological control.

**Assessment:** This critique is methodologically valid, and I partially concede it, though the severity is lower than critiques 1 and 2.

The critic is correct that the comparison as designed cannot cleanly attribute improvement to features. XGBoost can capture nonlinear interactions (age × comorbidity, utilization × LOS) that logistic regression cannot, and if these baseline-feature interactions are predictive, GBT on baseline features alone might outperform LR on baseline features substantially. In that scenario, any AUROC gap between the two reported models would be a mixture of feature gain and complexity gain, with no way to disentangle them from the reported results.

However, I want to note that the ablation structure described in Section 8 partially mitigates this: the 'baseline + embeddings only' ablation uses the GBT model family for the hypothesis configurations, so there is internal comparison across feature subsets within the same model family. The missing piece is specifically a GBT-on-baseline-features cell. This is a straightforward addition and should be included. The 'current clinical practice' justification is indeed a deployment framing and does not substitute for the methodological control.

Where I push back slightly: for the hypothesis being tested ('does adding sequential and note-derived features improve prediction over a clinically practical baseline?'), the LR comparison is not wrong — it just answers a different and more limited question. The richer comparison requires the missing cell. This is a design gap, not a fundamental validity failure, but it prevents clean causal attribution of any observed improvement.

**What remains uncertain:** The magnitude of the complexity confound. In many clinical prediction tasks, LR and GBT on the same baseline features perform comparably, which would make the confound small. But this is an empirical question that the current design cannot answer.

---

### Critique 4: Clinical BERT embedding assumptions

**Critic's claim (three sub-points):**
(a) The BERT model's training data provenance is undocumented; if it was trained on data temporally overlapping the study cohort (especially test-period notes), the embeddings carry forward-looking distributional information.
(b) Mean/max/percentile pooling discards sequential structure and conflates topic distribution with clinical trajectory.
(c) Note-length features may encode documentation-practice drift over the 3-year window rather than patient risk, and stratification by note length does not fully address this.

**Assessment:** These are valid concerns of varying severity.

*On (a) — BERT provenance:* This is a genuine documentation gap. The design should explicitly state which clinical BERT model is used (e.g., ClinicalBERT trained on MIMIC-III, a proprietary system model, BioBERT), its training data period, and whether that period overlaps the study cohort. If the BERT model was trained on notes from the same health system that overlap the test period, the embeddings for those notes will reflect in-distribution representations in a way that training-period notes may not, introducing an asymmetric advantage for the test set. This needs to be documented and, if overlap exists, addressed (e.g., by using a model trained strictly on external data or data predating the study window). I concede this is a real and fixable documentation/design gap.

*On (b) — Pooling discards trajectory information:* This is a valid limitation, but it is a limitation of expressiveness rather than a validity flaw. The design uses pooled statistics as a computationally tractable approximation; this choice makes the embeddings noisier but does not systematically bias AUROC upward or downward. I accept this as a named limitation that should be disclosed in the paper. The SHAP interpretation caveat the critic raises is correct — SHAP on pooled embeddings will not distinguish 'topic mentioned' from 'clinical trajectory changed' — and this limits interpretability claims, not predictive validity.

*On (c) — Note length and documentation drift:* The critic correctly identifies that note length is correlated with admission complexity and may also encode documentation culture changes over the study window. The stratified analysis by note length tests whether high-note-length and low-note-length subgroups show similar AUROC, which is a partial control. The critic's suggestion to residualize note length out of the embedding features (rather than just stratifying) is a stronger test and would provide better evidence. I concede that the current stratification approach is insufficient to rule out documentation-practice drift as a confound for the embedding features.

**What remains uncertain:** The practical impact of each of these. The BERT provenance issue is fixable with documentation and, if needed, model substitution. The pooling limitation is acknowledged but does not constitute a validity threat to the AUROC estimate. The note-length confound risk is real but may be small if the 3-year window does not span major documentation culture changes at the health system.

---

## Pass 2 — Verdict Selection

Summarizing the Pass 1 findings:

- **Critique 1 (preprocessing leakage):** Conceded. A real methodological flaw that biases the test AUROC upward by an unknown but potentially meaningful amount. Fixable but requires re-running the pipeline.
- **Critique 2 (test set selection):** Conceded. A structural violation of held-out test set integrity that makes the bootstrapped confidence intervals anti-conservative. Requires a design change (strict test set embargo or a second holdout).
- **Critique 3 (complexity confound):** Partially conceded. A missing control cell (GBT on baseline features) prevents clean attribution of improvement to features vs. complexity. This is a design gap but not a fundamental validity failure for the predictive question.
- **Critique 4 (BERT assumptions):** Partially conceded on provenance documentation and note-length confound; the pooling limitation is named as an expressiveness constraint rather than a validity flaw.

Two of the four critiques are genuine critical flaws (Critiques 1 and 2) that together mean the reported test AUROC is biased upward and does not represent an unbiased generalization estimate. Per the calibration rule, conceding multiple critical flaws requires a verdict of `empirical_test_agreed` or `critique_wins`. Since the flaws are fixable (fit preprocessing on training only; enforce strict test set embargo or use a second holdout) and the core experimental structure — temporal split, confound controls, calibration targets — is sound, the design is not irreparably broken. The disagreement over how much the AUROC estimate is inflated, and whether the corrected pipeline would still show ≥5pp improvement, requires empirical re-execution rather than argument. Verdict: `empirical_test_agreed`.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers (scalers, imputers, vocabularies) are fitted on the full cohort including the test period, introducing temporal leakage into every feature derived from those transformers.

The test set is used to break ties between model configurations before being re-used as the primary evaluation surface, making the reported AUROC conditional on test-set-based selection and therefore anti-conservative.

The baseline model (logistic regression) and hypothesis model (GBT) differ in both feature set and model family, making it impossible to attribute AUROC improvement to features vs. model complexity without a GBT-on-baseline-features control.

The clinical BERT model's training data provenance is undocumented, risking temporal overlap with the study cohort; mean/max pooling of embeddings conflates topic distribution with clinical trajectory; note-length features may encode documentation-practice drift rather than patient risk.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fitted on the full cohort (including test period) introduce temporal leakage into feature construction, biasing reported AUROC upward by an unknown amount — conceded by the defender with no adequate rebuttal.

The test set is used to break ties between model configurations before being re-used as the primary evaluation surface, making the reported AUROC and bootstrapped confidence intervals anti-conservative — conceded by the defender as a structural flaw with no adequate rebuttal.
Absence of a GBT-on-baseline-features control cell prevents attribution of AUROC improvement to feature richness versus model complexity — partially conceded and unresolved.

Clinical BERT training data provenance is undocumented (risking temporal overlap with the study cohort), and note-length stratification is insufficient to rule out documentation-practice drift as a confound for embedding features — partially conceded and unresolved.
